subject: Re: Closely spaced (helical) particles
body:
Also interested in info on this, my use case is densely packed lattices and I’ve been a bit worried about the possibility of M locking onto neighbouring lattice positions during refinement.

Cheers,

Alister

> On 25 Jan 2021, at 05:19, Charles Sindelar <charles.sindelar@yale.edu> wrote:
> 
> ﻿Hello Dimitry, I have been trying to use M/WARP on a tomography data set of filaments, even though I am aware this is "bleeding edge" stuff for the current software version.
> 
> I have managed to run some M refinement cycles. However, when I look in the species folder at the star files, I can see that the particle coordinates and euler angles are not changing. There is some evolution of stage warping parameters, it appears, although I'm not sure this is trustworthy.
> 
> The way I set up the M run was :
> (1) Refined helical particle coordinates (from RELION) have been carefully regularized as ideal filaments- no duplicates or gaps, and subunits are spaced apart by the natural repeat distance of 52 Å. There is also almost no twist (these are highly curved, however, and form supercoils of 200nm diameter).
> (2) I gave a particle diameter of 400Å, and defined a tight mask around the tube, of about the same length.
> 
> I can imagine easily getting stuck in local energy landscape minima with a setup like this, but obviously it would depends on internal details of M that I can't seem to deduce from the paper/docs. I am hoping to find workarounds to basically "trick" M into doing the right thing by choosing the right input parameters. 
> 
> Do you have suggestions? How exactly is the "particle diameter" used within the M algorithm? If particles are closer together than the mask radius, how does this affect the refinement target function?
> 
> Hope this is not too elaborate of a question! Your thoughts much appreciated, Chuck
> 
> --
